Land Rover Discovery Sport P200 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Discovery Sport P200 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 204PT engine typically costs £3,000–£5,000 supply only, with the same engine used across the Discovery Sport, Evoque L551, and E-Pace. Fitting adds £650–£1,100. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Discovery Sport P200 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 204PT Ingenium petrol is considered significantly more reliable than its diesel sibling (204DTD). It's rated ★★★★☆ based on current data, but cooling system maintenance — particularly the water pump — is the key to longevity. No catastrophic design flaws have emerged.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Discovery Sport P200?

acceptedAnswer.text: Water pump failure, coolant crossover pipe leaks, and occasional timing chain tensioner wear are the most common issues. None typically lead to full engine replacement if caught early — preventative cooling system maintenance is the priority.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Discovery Sport P200 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a £10,000+ Discovery Sport P200, a £4,200 recon engine is a manageable 42% of the vehicle's value — worth it for a well-maintained example. Given the Discovery Sport's strong retained values, replacement is usually viable. Scrap only if the vehicle has suffered catastrophic damage well beyond the engine.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Discovery Sport's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: For the P200, the engine code is 204PT — it's on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The P200 badge signifies the 2.0-litre turbocharged petrol Ingenium. If you're unsure, tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the 204PT's water pump, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the water pump is a straightforward repair costing £400–£800. The 204PT's water pump is the most common service item on this engine; replacing it as a preventative measure at 60,000 miles is strongly recommended.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Discovery Sport P200 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — replace the pump before it fails; far cheaper than overheating damage
If caught early; don't drive with a rattling chain
Straightforward part replacement; book at next service
If head gasket or block damage has occurred, replacement may be cheaper
Not a common 204PT failure; if it occurs, replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Discovery Sport P200? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Land Rover service history with documented oil-change intervals
* Water pump replaced at or before 60,000 miles as a preventative measure
* Coolant crossover pipes inspected for signs of brittleness or leaks
* Post-2021 build with any early cooling system software updates applied
avoidIf: * Service history shows repeated coolant top-ups with no diagnosis
* Any evidence of overheating — check the service records for head gasket work
* No record of cooling system maintenance on higher-mileage examples (60k+)
* Engine warning light present with no diagnosis documentation

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Discovery Sport P200's 204PT is the most reliable Ingenium variant — a genuine step forward from the diesel 204DTD. But like all modern engines, it demands the right cooling system maintenance. Buy on service history and proof of water pump replacement. If you're replacing, a reconditioned unit with a 12-month warranty is the sweet spot.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Discovery Sport P200 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle on startup (timing chain tensioner wear)
* Service History — Including which engine era (204PT is the only P200 engine — verify this before purchase)
* Coolant Level — Is the coolant low with no visible leak? Potential water pump or crossover pipe issue
* Turbo Boost — Test acceleration; any hesitation, lag, or whistling?
* Cooling System Check — Has the water pump been replaced? This is the number‑one preventative item
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 5,000 miles? If so, investigate
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved cooling or fuelling issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures; petrol engines should pass easily if well‑maintained

items.label: Most Common Failure
